question: [File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileServiceImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByTimestamp(Long timestamp)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a crafted timestamp value equal to the consensusEnd of a target transaction's record file, exploiting the `consensusEnd >= ?1` query in RecordFileRepository to retrieve a different (later) record file than intended, causing the caller to process transactions from the wrong block and enabling transaction context confusion?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileServiceImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByTimestamp(Long timestamp)] Can an unprivileged user supply a timestamp of 0L to findByTimestamp(), causing the query `consensusEnd >= 0` to always return the earliest record file regardless of the actual transaction timestamp, allowing the attacker to anchor all transaction lookups to genesis-era state and tamper with the effective block context of submitted transactions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileServiceImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByTimestamp(Long timestamp)] Can an unprivileged user supply Long.MAX_VALUE as a timestamp to findByTimestamp(), causing the `consensusEnd >= Long.MAX_VALUE` query to return an empty Optional, and if the caller does not handle the empty result defensively, trigger a NullPointerException or fallback to a default block that processes the submitted transaction under incorrect state, leading to transaction tampering impact?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileServiceImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByTimestamp(Long timestamp)] Can an unprivileged user exploit the @CachePut annotation on findByTimestamp() — which writes the result into the CACHE_MANAGER_RECORD_FILE_INDEX cache keyed by index — to poison the index cache with a stale or attacker-chosen RecordFile, so that a subsequent call to findByIndex() for the same index returns the poisoned entry, causing submitted transactions to be evaluated against tampered block data?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileServiceImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByBlockType(BlockType block)] Can an unprivileged user pass the string 'pending' as a block type, which maps to BlockType.LATEST (Long.MAX_VALUE) via the switch in BlockType.of(), causing findByBlockType() to call findLatest() and return the most recent record file, allowing the attacker to force transaction simulation against the latest unfinalized state and tamper with the effective execution context of submitted transactions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileServiceImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByBlockType(BlockType block)] Can an unprivileged user pass the string 'safe' or 'finalized' as a block type, both of which silently map to BlockType.LATEST in BlockType.of(), causing findByBlockType() to return the latest record file instead of a finalized/safe checkpoint, allowing the attacker to submit transactions that appear to execute against a finalized block but actually execute against the latest mutable state, enabling state confusion attacks?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileServiceImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByBlockType(BlockType block)] Can an unprivileged user pass an empty string to BlockType.of(), which returns BlockType.LATEST per the StringUtils.isEmpty() check, causing findByBlockType() to silently resolve to the latest block, allowing the attacker to bypass explicit block selection and force transaction execution against the latest state without specifying a block, tampering with the intended historical execution context?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileServiceImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByBlockType(BlockType block)] Can an unprivileged user supply a hex-encoded block number via BlockType.of() with the '0x' prefix that, after parsing with Long.parseLong(cleanedValue, 16), overflows or wraps to a negative long value (e.g., '0x8000000000000000'), causing findByIndex() to be called with a negative index and returning an empty Optional or unexpected record file, leading to transaction execution against an undefined block state?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileServiceImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByBlockType(BlockType block)] Can an unprivileged user supply a very large decimal block number (e.g., Long.MAX_VALUE as a string) to BlockType.of(), causing extractNumericBlock() to parse it as a valid long and call findByIndex(Long.MAX_VALUE), which returns an empty Optional, and if the caller does not handle the empty result, trigger a fallback to an incorrect block context for submitted transaction processing?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileServiceImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByBlockType(BlockType block)] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that BlockType.LATEST has number=Long.MAX_VALUE, and if any downstream code performs arithmetic on block.number() (e.g., block.number() + 1), trigger a long overflow to Long.MIN_VALUE, causing the transaction to be evaluated against a completely wrong block index and enabling transaction tampering through arithmetic overflow?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileServiceImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByTimestamp(Long timestamp)] Can an unprivileged user exploit the @Caching annotation on findByTimestamp() — which combines @Cacheable (timestamp cache) and @CachePut (index cache) — to create a cache key collision where two different timestamps resolve to the same RecordFile index, causing a subsequent findByIndex() call to return a stale cached entry and process submitted transactions against the wrong block?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileServiceImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByTimestamp(Long timestamp)] Can an unprivileged user send a timestamp that is exactly 1 nanosecond before the consensusEnd of a target record file, exploiting the `consensusEnd >= ?1` (greater-than-or-equal) boundary in the JPQL query, to retrieve the target record file and associate a submitted transaction with that specific block, enabling precise block-context manipulation for transaction tampering?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileServiceImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByTimestamp(Long timestamp)] Can an unprivileged user supply a negative Long timestamp (e.g., -1L) to findByTimestamp(), which passes the Long parameter without validation in RecordFileServiceImpl, causing the underlying JPQL query `consensusEnd >= -1` to return the earliest available record file, allowing the attacker to anchor transaction execution to genesis state and tamper with the block context of submitted transactions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileServiceImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByBlockType(BlockType block)] Can an unprivileged user pass a null BlockType to findByBlockType(), bypassing the if/else-if chain (which only checks for EARLIEST and LATEST by reference equality), causing the code to fall through to recordFileRepository.findByIndex(block.number()) and throw a NullPointerException, resulting in a denial-of-service for transaction processing?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileServiceImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByBlockType(BlockType block)] Can an unprivileged user craft a BlockType with number=0 (not the EARLIEST singleton) by calling BlockType.of('0'), causing findByBlockType() to call findByIndex(0) instead of findEarliest(), bypassing the EARLIEST cache (CACHE_MANAGER_RECORD_FILE_EARLIEST) and hitting the index cache (CACHE_MANAGER_RECORD_FILE_INDEX) instead, potentially returning a different cached result and causing transaction context inconsistency?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileServiceImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByBlockType(BlockType block)] Can an unprivileged user exploit the reference equality check `block == BlockType.EARLIEST` in findByBlockType() — since BlockType is a record and new instances are not the same reference as the static constant — by constructing a BlockType with name='earliest' and number=0 via BlockType.of('earliest'), which does return the EARLIEST singleton, but if a caller constructs a new BlockType directly, the check fails and findByIndex(0) is called instead of findEarliest(), causing cache bypass and potential stale data for transaction processing?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileServiceImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByTimestamp(Long timestamp)] Can an unprivileged user exploit the cache TTL of CACHE_MANAGER_RECORD_FILE_TIMESTAMP by sending a timestamp lookup that caches a RecordFile result, then waiting for the underlying database to be updated with new transactions, and replaying the same timestamp to receive the stale cached RecordFile, causing submitted transactions to be evaluated against outdated block state and enabling transaction replay or context manipulation?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileServiceImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByBlockType(BlockType block)] Can an unprivileged user exploit the CACHE_MANAGER_RECORD_FILE_LATEST cache TTL by calling findByBlockType(BlockType.LATEST) to cache the current latest block, then submitting a transaction that should be evaluated against a newer block, but receiving the stale cached latest block due to cache not yet expiring, causing the transaction to be processed against an outdated state and enabling transaction tampering through cache staleness?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileServiceImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByBlockType(BlockType block)] Can an unprivileged user supply a hex block number with leading zeros after '0x' (e.g., '0x0000000000000001') to BlockType.of(), causing extractNumericBlock() to parse it as block 1, and if the cache key is based on the raw string value rather than the normalized number, bypass the cache for block 1 and force a fresh database query, enabling cache bypass attacks that could expose timing information about transaction processing?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileServiceImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByTimestamp(Long timestamp)] Can an unprivileged user send a high-frequency burst of findByTimestamp() calls with unique timestamp values that all miss the cache (unless='#result == null' means null results are not cached), causing repeated database queries for the `consensusEnd >= ?1` query, exhausting database connection pool resources and denying transaction processing service to legitimate users?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileServiceImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByBlockType(BlockType block)] Can an unprivileged user send a high-frequency burst of findByBlockType() calls with unique large block numbers that all miss the CACHE_MANAGER_RECORD_FILE_INDEX cache (since results are empty/null and unless='#result == null' prevents caching), causing repeated database queries and exhausting connection pool resources, denying transaction processing to legitimate users?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileServiceImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByTimestamp(Long timestamp)] Can an unprivileged user exploit the `unless = '#result == null'` cache condition on findByTimestamp() — which does not cache empty Optional results — by repeatedly querying timestamps that fall in gaps between record files (no matching consensusEnd), causing every such request to hit the database, enabling a cache-miss amplification DoS that degrades transaction processing throughput?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileServiceImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByBlockType(BlockType block)] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that 'pending', 'safe', and 'finalized' all map to BlockType.LATEST to submit a transaction with block tag 'pending' and have it execute against the same state as 'finalized', violating the semantic distinction between pending (unconfirmed) and finalized (confirmed) blocks, and allowing the attacker to observe finalized state while claiming to operate on pending transactions, enabling front-running or state-probing attacks?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileServiceImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByTimestamp(Long timestamp)] Can an unprivileged user exploit the @CachePut on findByTimestamp() that writes to CACHE_MANAGER_RECORD_FILE_INDEX to overwrite a legitimately cached index entry with a different RecordFile returned by the timestamp query, causing subsequent findByIndex() calls to return the wrong RecordFile and process submitted transactions against incorrect block data, constituting a cache poisoning attack on transaction integrity?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileServiceImpl.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByBlockType(BlockType block)] Can an unprivileged user supply a block number string with Unicode or special characters that pass the negative-number check in extractNumericBlock() but cause Long.parseLong() to throw an exception, which is caught and rethrown as IllegalArgumentException, and if the caller does not handle this exception, cause an unhandled error that disrupts transaction processing for all concurrent users sharing the same thread pool?
